subject: Psychology
topic: Cycle A Foundations — What Is Psychology?

instructional_contract.learning_target: Students explain psychology as the study of mind and behaviour, and practise using evidence to explain and sometimes predict human behaviour.
do: use evidence to support an explanation
instructional_contract.understand: Psychology helps us explain and sometimes predict human behaviour using evidence.

content.subtitle: Psychology helps us explain and sometimes predict human behaviour using evidence.
bullets: mind
behaviour
evidence
explanation
prediction
limits

slides.title: We already try to explain people

content.scenario: Every day, we guess why people do what they do.
prompts: Why did they leave me on read?
Why did I freeze during the presentation?
Why do I procrastinate even when I care?
Why do groups act differently than one person alone?

content.scenario: Psychology is not mind-reading, diagnosing classmates, personality-quiz entertainment, or saying ‘I just know.’
prompts: Observe behaviour.
Ask better questions.
Test explanations.
Use evidence.
Name limits.

content.subtitle: Behaviour is what we can see. The mind is what we are trying to understand.
content.caption: Behaviour gives us evidence, but not the whole answer.

bullets: Situation: you send a message. They see it. No reply.
Quick guess: ‘They must be mad at me.’
Other possibilities: busy, phone died, forgot, did not know what to say.
Limit: one message does not prove the reason.

slides.title: What did we notice? What did we guess?

content.model: Good explanations keep observations and inferences apart.
supports: Observation = what we can point to.
Inference = what we think might be going on.

bullets: Case evidence: a student misses two presentation days and looks tense when the topic comes up.
Possible explanation: anxiety or stress.
Possible explanation: low preparation or incomplete work.
Possible explanation: a past experience or outside pressure.
Limit: we cannot diagnose from this.

content.left: Weak: ‘They are just lazy.’ Problem: label, no evidence, no other possibilities.
content.right: Stronger: ‘They may be avoiding because of stress, low preparation, or a past experience. We need more evidence.’
prompts: Which version explains behaviour more carefully?
Which version avoids a whole-person label?

bullets: Hypothesis: a testable idea or prediction.
Evidence: information that helps us check an explanation.
Theory: a larger explanation supported over time.
Limit: what we cannot know yet.
Multiple causes: behaviour usually has more than one influence.

content.caption: A guess gets stronger when it can be checked.

slides.title: Many causes, not one simple label

content.scenario: Behaviour can be shaped by what is happening inside a person, around a person, and over time.
prompts: Inside: emotion, attention, memory, sleep, motivation.
Around: family, friends, school, culture, expectations.
Across time: past learning, habits, practice, stress, recent events.

bullets: No diagnosing classmates.
No whole-person labels from one behaviour.
Behaviour is not the same as identity.
Context matters.
Evidence matters.

content.scenario: Fix this sentence so it sounds more psychological: ‘She does not care about school.’
prompts: What can we actually see?
What might explain it?
What evidence would help?
What can we not know yet?
Psychology studies the ______ and ______.
Give one example of behaviour.
Give one example of something in the mind.
Turn ‘He is just lazy’ into a stronger explanation.
content.caption: A strong answer uses evidence and avoids a whole-person label.

rows: Psychology helps us explain and sometimes predict behaviour.
Psychology is not mind-reading or diagnosing classmates.
One behaviour can have many possible causes.
content.caption: Next: how psychologists study behaviour and test ideas.
